1	package ir
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"encoding/json"
6		"errors"
7		"fmt"
8		"io"
9		"sort"
10		"strings"
11	
12		"github.com/goccy/go-yaml"
13	)
14	
15	// Frontmatter is the recognized metadata block for a Node. The decoder
16	// extracts it from the source file and applies defaults for any field the
17	// author didn't set.
18	//
19	// Field defaults match the spec:
20	//   - Required: false
21	//   - Targets:  empty slice (means "all adapters")
22	//   - Version:  1
23	type Frontmatter struct {
24		Required bool     `yaml:"required" json:"__aienvs_required"`
25		Targets  []string `yaml:"targets"  json:"__aienvs_targets"`
26		Version  int      `yaml:"version"  json:"__aienvs_version"`
27	}
28	
29	// defaultFrontmatter returns a fresh Frontmatter with the documented
30	// defaults applied. Targets is an empty (non-nil) slice so the shape is
31	// stable regardless of source format (YAML/JSON/TOML); the spec contract
32	// is "empty == all adapters".
33	func defaultFrontmatter() Frontmatter {
34		return Frontmatter{
35			Required: false,
36			Targets:  []string{},
37			Version:  1,
38		}
39	}
40	
41	// applyDefaults fills in zero-value fields with documented defaults. Used
42	// after parsing a partial frontmatter block. Normalizes Targets to a
43	// non-nil empty slice so the shape is consistent across all extractors
44	// (a parsed YAML doc that omits `targets:` and a JSON file with no
45	// `__aienvs_targets` key both produce `[]string{}`, never nil).
46	func (fm *Frontmatter) applyDefaults() {
47		if fm.Version == 0 {
48			fm.Version = 1
49		}
50		if fm.Targets == nil {
51			fm.Targets = []string{}
52		}
53	}
54	
55	// extractMarkdownFrontmatter splits a markdown source into (frontmatter,
56	// body). When the source has no leading `---\n` block, returns
57	// defaultFrontmatter and the input unchanged.
58	//
59	// Frontmatter syntax: `---\n` on its own line, YAML, `---\n` on its own
60	// line. CR/LF tolerated. Unknown YAML fields are errors except the `x-`
61	// prefix (forward-compat).
62	func extractMarkdownFrontmatter(src []byte) (Frontmatter, []byte, error) {
63		const sep = "---"
64	
65		// Quick check: source must start with `---` followed by a newline.
66		if !startsWithDelimiter(src, sep) {
67			fm := defaultFrontmatter()
68			return fm, src, nil
69		}
70	
71		// Skip the opening delimiter line.
72		rest := skipFirstLine(src)
73	
74		// Find the closing `---` delimiter on its own line.
75		closeIdx := findDelimiterLine(rest, sep)
76		if closeIdx < 0 {
77			return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: missing closing --- delimiter", ErrFrontmatterParse)
78		}
79	
80		yamlBytes := rest[:closeIdx]
81		body := skipFirstLine(rest[closeIdx:])
82	
83		fm, err := parseYAMLFrontmatter(yamlBytes)
84		if err != nil {
85			return Frontmatter{}, nil, err
86		}
87		fm.applyDefaults()
88		return fm, body, nil
89	}
90	
91	// extractJSONFrontmatter parses a JSON file, peels off any reserved
92	// `__aienvs_*` top-level keys, and returns a Frontmatter plus a re-emitted
93	// body with those keys stripped. Non-aienvs keys preserve their original
94	// ordering and structure (encoding/json's map ordering is sorted, which
95	// is what JSON consumers should already tolerate).
96	//
97	// When the body fails to parse as JSON, returns ErrFrontmatterParse — the
98	// canonical-repo file isn't valid JSON, period.
99	func extractJSONFrontmatter(src []byte) (Frontmatter, []byte, error) {
100		if len(bytes.TrimSpace(src)) == 0 {
101			return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: empty JSON body", ErrFrontmatterParse)
102		}
103	
104		// Decode as a generic map so we can pick out reserved keys without
105		// imposing a schema on the rest of the document.
106		var raw map[string]json.RawMessage
107		dec := json.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(src))
108		dec.UseNumber()
109		if err := dec.Decode(&raw); err != nil {
110			return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: %w", ErrFrontmatterParse, err)
111		}
112		// Reject trailing data after the first value. The JSON decoder will
113		// happily stop at the end of the first object, so `{"a":1}{"b":2}` or
114		// `{"a":1} trailing` would silently lose everything past the first
115		// brace. For mcp/*.json, that masks merge-corrupted files; require
116		// EOF (modulo whitespace) to ensure end-to-end validity.
117		var probe json.RawMessage
118		if err := dec.Decode(&probe); !errors.Is(err, io.EOF) {
119			if err == nil {
120				return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: trailing data after JSON object", ErrFrontmatterParse)
121			}
122			return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: trailing data after JSON object: %w", ErrFrontmatterParse, err)
123		}
124	
125		fm := defaultFrontmatter()
126	
127		// Reserved keys are best-effort: if the field exists but doesn't
128		// unmarshal cleanly into its target type, return ErrFrontmatterParse.
129		if v, ok := raw["__aienvs_required"]; ok {
130			if err := json.Unmarshal(v, &fm.Required); err != nil {
131				return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: __aienvs_required must be bool", ErrFrontmatterParse)
132			}
133			delete(raw, "__aienvs_required")
134		}
135		if v, ok := raw["__aienvs_targets"]; ok {
136			if err := json.Unmarshal(v, &fm.Targets); err != nil {
137				return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: __aienvs_targets must be []string", ErrFrontmatterParse)
138			}
139			delete(raw, "__aienvs_targets")
140		}
141		if v, ok := raw["__aienvs_version"]; ok {
142			if err := json.Unmarshal(v, &fm.Version); err != nil {
143				return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: __aienvs_version must be int", ErrFrontmatterParse)
144			}
145			delete(raw, "__aienvs_version")
146		}
147		fm.applyDefaults()
148	
149		// Re-emit the stripped body. Use deterministic key ordering so the
150		// output is byte-stable across decode runs (a determinism invariant).
151		body, err := marshalJSONStable(raw)
152		if err != nil {
153			return Frontmatter{}, nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: re-emit failed: %w", ErrFrontmatterParse, err)
154		}
155		return fm, body, nil
156	}
157	
158	// extractTOMLFrontmatter is a v1 stub: it returns default frontmatter and
159	// the body unchanged. Reserved-key extraction in TOML is deferred per
160	// docs/spec/ir-v1.md "Frontmatter on non-markdown nodes".
161	func extractTOMLFrontmatter(src []byte) (Frontmatter, []byte, error) {
162		return defaultFrontmatter(), src, nil
163	}
164	
165	// parseYAMLFrontmatter decodes the YAML block with strict unknown-field
166	// detection plus an `x-` prefix allowance for forward-compat experiments.
167	func parseYAMLFrontmatter(src []byte) (Frontmatter, error) {
168		if len(bytes.TrimSpace(src)) == 0 {
169			return defaultFrontmatter(), nil
170		}
171		var fm Frontmatter
172		err := yaml.UnmarshalWithOptions(
173			src,
174			&fm,
175			yaml.DisallowUnknownField(),
176			yaml.AllowFieldPrefixes("x-"),
177		)
178		if err != nil {
179			// Distinguish unknown-field errors so callers can surface
180			// ErrUnknownFrontmatterField specifically.
181			if isUnknownFieldErr(err) {
182				return Frontmatter{}, fmt.Errorf("%w: %s", ErrUnknownFrontmatterField, yaml.FormatError(err, false, true))
183			}
184			return Frontmatter{}, fmt.Errorf("%w: %s", ErrFrontmatterParse, yaml.FormatError(err, false, true))
185		}
186		return fm, nil
187	}
188	
189	// isUnknownFieldErr matches goccy/go-yaml's unknown-field error
190	// signature. The library doesn't expose a typed sentinel for this case at
191	// 1.19, so we fall back to a substring check on the formatted message.
192	// Imperfect but good enough for the test surface; if goccy adds a typed
193	// error in a later release we swap to errors.Is.
194	func isUnknownFieldErr(err error) bool {
195		if err == nil {
196			return false
197		}
198		msg := err.Error()
199		return bytes.Contains([]byte(msg), []byte("unknown field"))
200	}
201	
202	// startsWithDelimiter reports whether src begins with `<delim>` followed
203	// by `\n` or `\r\n` (CR/LF tolerant).
204	func startsWithDelimiter(src []byte, delim string) bool {
205		d := []byte(delim)
206		if !bytes.HasPrefix(src, d) {
207			return false
208		}
209		rest := src[len(d):]
210		switch {
211		case bytes.HasPrefix(rest, []byte("\n")):
212			return true
213		case bytes.HasPrefix(rest, []byte("\r\n")):
214			return true
215		}
216		return false
217	}
218	
219	// findDelimiterLine returns the byte offset of the next `<delim>` on its
220	// own line within src, or -1 if none is found. Accepts `\n`, `\r\n`, or
221	// EOF as line terminators after the delimiter. A bare `\r` (Mac-classic
222	// line endings) is rejected — if it isn't followed by `\n` we don't
223	// treat it as a closing delimiter line, matching the spec which only
224	// recognizes Unix and Windows line endings.
225	func findDelimiterLine(src []byte, delim string) int {
226		d := []byte(delim)
227		offset := 0
228		for offset < len(src) {
229			// Locate the next occurrence at the start of a line.
230			idx := bytes.Index(src[offset:], d)
231			if idx < 0 {
232				return -1
233			}
234			abs := offset + idx
235			// Must be at start-of-line: either offset 0, or preceded by `\n`.
236			atLineStart := abs == 0 || src[abs-1] == '\n'
237			// Must be followed by `\n`, a full `\r\n` sequence, or EOF.
238			afterDelim := abs + len(d)
239			var atLineEnd bool
240			switch {
241			case afterDelim == len(src):
242				atLineEnd = true
243			case src[afterDelim] == '\n':
244				atLineEnd = true
245			case src[afterDelim] == '\r':
246				// `\r` is only valid as the lead byte of `\r\n`. A trailing
247				// bare `\r` (or `\r` followed by anything else) is rejected
248				// rather than silently mis-splitting frontmatter and body.
249				atLineEnd = afterDelim+1 < len(src) && src[afterDelim+1] == '\n'
250			}
251			if atLineStart && atLineEnd {
252				return abs
253			}
254			offset = abs + 1
255		}
256		return -1
257	}
258	
259	// skipFirstLine returns src with the first line (up to and including the
260	// first \n) removed. If there is no newline, returns an empty slice.
261	func skipFirstLine(src []byte) []byte {
262		idx := bytes.IndexByte(src, '\n')
263		if idx < 0 {
264			return src[len(src):]
265		}
266		return src[idx+1:]
267	}
268	
269	// marshalJSONStable serializes a map[string]json.RawMessage with sorted
270	// keys. encoding/json already sorts map keys, but we go via Marshal
271	// here for clarity and to make the determinism contract explicit.
272	func marshalJSONStable(m map[string]json.RawMessage) ([]byte, error) {
273		keys := make([]string, 0, len(m))
274		for k := range m {
275			keys = append(keys, k)
276		}
277		sort.Strings(keys)
278	
279		var buf bytes.Buffer
280		buf.WriteByte('{')
281		for i, k := range keys {
282			if i > 0 {
283				buf.WriteByte(',')
284			}
285			// Encode the key as JSON to handle escaping.
286			kb, err := json.Marshal(k)
287			if err != nil {
288				return nil, err
289			}
290			buf.Write(kb)
291			buf.WriteByte(':')
292			buf.Write(m[k])
293		}
294		buf.WriteByte('}')
295		return buf.Bytes(), nil
296	}
297	
298	// kindForExt maps a file extension to its expected Kind. Returns ok=false
299	// for extensions outside the IR-owned vocabulary.
300	func kindForExt(ext string) (Kind, bool) {
301		switch ext {
302		case ".md":
303			return KindRule, true // also covers AgentsMD/Command/Skill SKILL.md by directory
304		case ".json":
305			return KindMCPServerEntry, true
306		case ".toml":
307			return KindPluginReference, true
308		}
309		return "", false
310	}
311	
312	// validateTargets ensures every entry in targets matches a known adapter
313	// name. The decoder receives `knownTargets` from its caller (the adapter
314	// registry from Unit 8). Empty targets means "all" and is always valid.
315	//
316	// Gating semantics match DecodeOptions: a `nil` registry means "no
317	// registry wired, accept anything" (tests + early v1). A non-nil but
318	// empty registry means "no adapters known, reject any declared target"
319	// — that is a real configuration the caller must surface, not a silent
320	// pass-through.
321	func validateTargets(targets []string, knownTargets map[string]struct{}) error {
322		if knownTargets == nil {
323			// Decoder was called without a target registry — accept whatever
324			// the file declared. Useful for tests and for the v1 case where
325			// adapters haven't been wired yet.
326			return nil
327		}
328		for _, t := range targets {
329			if _, ok := knownTargets[t]; !ok {
330				return fmt.Errorf("%w: %q", ErrUnknownTarget, t)
331			}
332		}
333		return nil
334	}
335	
336	// extractFrontmatter is the dispatcher used by the decoder. It picks the
337	// right extractor based on the file's path/extension semantics:
338	//
339	//   - `.md`, `.markdown` → markdown frontmatter
340	//   - `.json`            → JSON reserved-key extraction
341	//   - `.toml`            → TOML stub (defaults only, v1)
342	//
343	// Returns ErrFrontmatterParse when the file format is recognized but the
344	// metadata extraction fails.
345	func extractFrontmatter(path string, src []byte) (Frontmatter, []byte, error) {
346		switch ext(path) {
347		case ".md", ".markdown":
348			return extractMarkdownFrontmatter(src)
349		case ".json":
350			return extractJSONFrontmatter(src)
351		case ".toml":
352			return extractTOMLFrontmatter(src)
353		}
354		// Unknown extensions don't have frontmatter — return defaults and the
355		// raw body.
356		return defaultFrontmatter(), src, nil
357	}
358	
359	// ext returns the lowercase extension including the leading dot, or "" if
360	// none. Mirrors filepath.Ext but kept local to avoid pulling filepath into
361	// the package's hot path. Canonical-repo paths are typically lowercase,
362	// but we lower-case defensively so `foo.MD`, `foo.JSON`, etc. still
363	// route to their owning extractor instead of falling through to the
364	// "no frontmatter" branch.
365	func ext(p string) string {
366		for i := len(p) - 1; i >= 0 && p[i] != '/'; i-- {
367			if p[i] == '.' {
368				return strings.ToLower(p[i:])
369			}
370		}
371		return ""
372	}
373	
374	// Note: ErrFrontmatterParse already wraps the parse error from goccy or
375	// json; callers don't need to walk the chain unless they want to display
376	// the underlying detail. errors.Is(err, ErrFrontmatterParse) holds in all
377	// frontmatter-parse failure paths.
378	var _ = errors.Is
379

1	// Package ir implements the v1 Intermediate Representation described in
2	// docs/spec/ir-v1.md.
3	//
4	// The decoder reads a canonical-repo at a specific commit SHA and returns
5	// a deterministic ordered slice of Nodes. Each adapter (claude, cursor,
6	// codex, …) consumes Nodes; no adapter ever sees raw canonical files.
7	//
8	// This package owns the IR types only. The decoder lives in decode.go and
9	// the per-concept wrappers in kinds.go.
10	package ir
11	
12	import (
13		"errors"
14		"fmt"
15		"regexp"
16	)
17	
18	// Kind enumerates the closed set of v1 concept kinds. Adding a kind is a
19	// v2-grade change.
20	type Kind string
21	
22	const (
23		KindAgentsMD        Kind = "agents-md"
24		KindRule            Kind = "rule"
25		KindSkill           Kind = "skill"
26		KindCommand         Kind = "command"
27		KindPluginReference Kind = "plugin-reference"
28		KindMCPServerEntry  Kind = "mcp-server-entry"
29	)
30	
31	// AllKinds returns the v1 closed set in stable order. Useful for capability
32	// matrix iteration and tests.
33	func AllKinds() []Kind {
34		return []Kind{
35			KindAgentsMD,
36			KindRule,
37			KindSkill,
38			KindCommand,
39			KindPluginReference,
40			KindMCPServerEntry,
41		}
42	}
43	
44	// ValidateKind reports nil when k is a recognized v1 kind.
45	func ValidateKind(k Kind) error {
46		for _, want := range AllKinds() {
47			if k == want {
48				return nil
49			}
50		}
51		return fmt.Errorf("ir: unrecognized kind %q", string(k))
52	}
53	
54	// Node is one IR record produced by the decoder. See docs/spec/ir-v1.md for
55	// the full contract.
56	type Node struct {
57		// ID is the canonical identifier for this node. Pattern: [a-z0-9][a-z0-9-_]{0,63}.
58		// Uniqueness is enforced per Kind, not globally.
59		ID string
60	
61		// Kind is one of the six v1 concept kinds.
62		Kind Kind
63	
64		// Version is the author-controlled monotonic counter from frontmatter.
65		// Default is 1 when frontmatter is absent or omits the field.
66		Version int
67	
68		// Required, when true, means every targeted adapter MUST mark this Kind
69		// as `supported` in its capability matrix. A required-unmet target
70		// causes sync to fail.
71		Required bool
72	
73		// Targets restricts which adapters see this node. Empty means "all
74		// registered adapters".
75		Targets []string
76	
77		// Provenance points back to the canonical-repo file the node came from.
78		Provenance Provenance
79	
80		// Body is the post-frontmatter content for markdown kinds, or the full
81		// (post-`__aienvs_*`-strip) file content for JSON/TOML kinds.
82		Body []byte
83	}
84	
85	// Provenance is the git-keyed location of the source file in the canonical
86	// repo. BlobSHA is the SHA git itself computed for the file content; it
87	// lets ledger / validate / diff cross-check against `git cat-file`.
88	type Provenance struct {
89		// Path is the posix-style path within the canonical repo (e.g.
90		// "rules/no-pr-on-friday.md").
91		Path string
92	
93		// BlobSHA is the 40-hex git blob SHA.
94		BlobSHA string
95	}
96	
97	// Asset is an auxiliary blob carried alongside a Skill node. Skill
98	// directories may contain non-SKILL.md files; each becomes an Asset.
99	type Asset struct {
100		// RelPath is the path relative to the skill's directory (e.g.
101		// "templates/foo.txt" for skills/<id>/templates/foo.txt).
102		RelPath string
103	
104		// Provenance is the git location of the asset.
105		Provenance Provenance
106	
107		// Content is the asset's full byte contents.
108		Content []byte
109	}
110	
111	// Skill bundles a Node with its asset list. Returned alongside the base
112	// Node slice via the decoder's SkillsByID accessor.
113	type Skill struct {
114		Node
115		Assets []Asset
116	}
117	
118	// Warning is a non-fatal observation from the decoder. The CLI surfaces
119	// warnings in the sync report; they do not fail decode.
120	type Warning struct {
121		Code    string
122		Message string
123	
124		// Provenance points to the offending file when one is known. Zero
125		// value when the warning is global (e.g., "AGENTS.md missing").
126		Provenance Provenance
127	}
128	
129	// Sentinel errors. Callers branch with errors.Is.
130	var (
131		// ErrUnrecognizedFile is returned for files inside an IR-owned
132		// directory whose extension does not match the kind (e.g., rules/foo.txt).
133		ErrUnrecognizedFile = errors.New("ir: unrecognized file in IR-owned directory")
134	
135		// ErrInvalidID is returned when a node id does not match the
136		// idPattern regex.
137		ErrInvalidID = errors.New("ir: node id does not match required pattern")
138	
139		// ErrDuplicateID is returned when two nodes of the same kind share an id.
140		ErrDuplicateID = errors.New("ir: duplicate node id within kind")
141	
142		// ErrUnknownTarget is returned when a frontmatter targets: entry names
143		// an adapter not in the registered set.
144		ErrUnknownTarget = errors.New("ir: targets references unknown adapter")
145	
146		// ErrFrontmatterParse is returned when the frontmatter block is
147		// malformed (no closing ---, invalid YAML, etc.).
148		ErrFrontmatterParse = errors.New("ir: frontmatter parse failed")
149	
150		// ErrUnknownFrontmatterField is returned when frontmatter contains a
151		// non-x- field outside the recognized set.
152		ErrUnknownFrontmatterField = errors.New("ir: unknown frontmatter field")
153	
154		// ErrSkillMissingSKILL is returned for a directory under skills/ that
155		// lacks a SKILL.md file.
156		ErrSkillMissingSKILL = errors.New("ir: skill directory missing SKILL.md")
157	
158		// ErrEmptyAgentsMD is returned when AGENTS.md is present but
159		// zero-length.
160		ErrEmptyAgentsMD = errors.New("ir: AGENTS.md is present but empty")
161	)
162	
163	// Warning codes. Spec freezes these strings; CLI / tests reference them.
164	const (
165		WarnAgentsMDMissing      = "agents-md-missing"
166		WarnSkillAssetUnreadable = "skill-asset-unreadable"
167	)
168	
169	// idPattern is the v1 id grammar: leading alphanumeric, then up to 63 of
170	// [a-z0-9-_]. Total length 1..64.
171	var idPattern = regexp.MustCompile(`\A[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,63}\z`)
172	
173	// IsValidID reports whether id matches the v1 grammar.
174	func IsValidID(id string) bool {
175		return idPattern.MatchString(id)
176	}
177

1	// Package capmatrix owns the capability-status types used by the adapter
2	// framework. Each adapter declares a per-Kind support level via
3	// `capabilities.yaml` (Unit 8); the framework merges those declarations
4	// into a workspace-wide matrix at sync time.
5	//
6	// This package is intentionally tiny in v1: the type, validation, and a
7	// small merge helper. It does not import internal/ir to avoid an import
8	// cycle — adapters import both packages.
9	package capmatrix
10	
11	import "fmt"
12	
13	// CapabilityStatus is the per-Kind support level an adapter declares.
14	type CapabilityStatus string
15	
16	const (
17		// Supported: adapter fully implements this Kind.
18		Supported CapabilityStatus = "supported"
19	
20		// Partial: adapter implements the Kind but with documented limits
21		// (e.g., only some frontmatter fields). Required nodes mapped to
22		// Partial succeed but emit a `warning` op.
23		Partial CapabilityStatus = "partial"
24	
25		// Unsupported: adapter does not implement this Kind. Required nodes
26		// mapped to Unsupported cause sync to fail with required_unmet.
27		Unsupported CapabilityStatus = "unsupported"
28	)
29	
30	// AllStatuses returns the v1 closed set in stable order.
31	func AllStatuses() []CapabilityStatus {
32		return []CapabilityStatus{Supported, Partial, Unsupported}
33	}
34	
35	// Validate reports nil when s is one of the documented statuses.
36	//
37	// Capability strings outside this set are decode errors at adapter-load
38	// time (Unit 8 wires this).
39	func Validate(s CapabilityStatus) error {
40		switch s {
41		case Supported, Partial, Unsupported:
42			return nil
43		default:
44			return fmt.Errorf("capmatrix: unrecognized status %q (want supported|partial|unsupported)", string(s))
45		}
46	}
47	
48	// Compare returns -1, 0, +1 for a strict-to-loose ordering used when the
49	// framework needs the "weakest" support level across multiple adapters
50	// targeting the same node:
51	//
52	//	Unsupported (-1) < Partial (0) < Supported (+1)
53	//
54	// Higher value = stronger support. Use Min(a, b) to find the limiting
55	// adapter.
56	func Compare(a, b CapabilityStatus) int {
57		return rank(a) - rank(b)
58	}
59	
60	// Min returns the weaker of a and b. Used to compute the effective support
61	// level for a node when multiple adapters are targeted.
62	func Min(a, b CapabilityStatus) CapabilityStatus {
63		if Compare(a, b) <= 0 {
64			return a
65		}
66		return b
67	}
68	
69	// rank maps a status to its sort key. Unknown statuses sort below
70	// Unsupported so they fail required-checks the same way.
71	func rank(s CapabilityStatus) int {
72		switch s {
73		case Supported:
74			return 2
75		case Partial:
76			return 1
77		case Unsupported:
78			return 0
79		default:
80			return -1
81		}
82	}
83

1	// Package adapter is the runtime layer of Unit 8: subprocess and
2	// in-process orchestration that turns the wire types in
3	// internal/adapter/contract into runnable adapter sessions.
4	//
5	// The package owns:
6	//   - adapter.yaml manifest parsing (this file)
7	//   - adapter discovery (PATH + workspace-manifest + bundled)
8	//   - subprocess and in-process transports
9	//   - the lifecycle orchestrator (initialize → initialized → emit → shutdown)
10	//   - declared-outputs integrity gate enforcement
11	//
12	// The wire types live in internal/adapter/contract; this package depends
13	// on them but does not re-export them.
14	package adapter
15	
16	import (
17		"errors"
18		"fmt"
19		"os"
20		"regexp"
21		"strings"
22	
23		"github.com/goccy/go-yaml"
24	
25		"github.com/aienvs/aienvs/internal/manifest"
26	)
27	
28	// MaxAdapterManifestBytes caps the on-disk size of an adapter.yaml.
29	// 1 MiB is generous: real manifests are well under 1 KiB, and the cap
30	// is there to defend against pathological or hostile files.
31	const MaxAdapterManifestBytes = 1 << 20
32	
33	// ContractVersionV1 is the only contract_version this build of aienvs
34	// recognizes. Future protocol versions land via Unit 8b under capability
35	// negotiation; the wire form itself stays at aienvs/v1.
36	const ContractVersionV1 = "aienvs/v1"
37	
38	// AdapterManifest describes one adapter as declared by its on-disk
39	// adapter.yaml. The manifest sits next to the binary on PATH and
40	// supplies the runtime with the metadata it needs to spawn the adapter
41	// and route ops correctly.
42	type AdapterManifest struct {
43		// Name is the adapter's identifier. Must be a single token of
44		// [a-z0-9][a-z0-9-_]{0,63} — same shape as IR node ids. Used in
45		// PATH lookups (aienvs-adapter-<name>) and in the workspace
46		// manifest's adapters: block.
47		Name string `yaml:"name"`
48	
49		// Version is a free-form version string. Compared against by humans;
50		// the runtime does not interpret it.
51		Version string `yaml:"version,omitempty"`
52	
53		// ContractVersion is the wire-protocol version the adapter speaks.
54		// Must equal ContractVersionV1; the runtime refuses to load adapters
55		// declaring any other value.
56		ContractVersion string `yaml:"contract_version"`
57	
58		// Command is the argv slice the runtime uses to spawn the adapter.
59		// Must be non-empty. The first element is resolved via $PATH unless
60		// it contains a path separator.
61		Command []string `yaml:"command"`
62	
63		// ReservedPrefix is the path prefix (relative to the workspace root)
64		// the adapter owns. The runtime uses this to detect nested-prefix
65		// violations across adapters and to scope the declared-outputs gate.
66		// Trailing slashes are stripped on load.
67		ReservedPrefix string `yaml:"reserved_prefix,omitempty"`
68	}
69	
70	// Manifest-level sentinel errors. Callers branch with errors.Is.
71	var (
72		// ErrAdapterManifest is returned for any adapter.yaml problem the
73		// caller hasn't already classified more specifically.
74		ErrAdapterManifest = errors.New("adapter: invalid adapter.yaml")
75	
76		// ErrAdapterManifestTooLarge is returned when the file size exceeds
77		// MaxAdapterManifestBytes.
78		ErrAdapterManifestTooLarge = errors.New("adapter: adapter.yaml exceeds maximum size")
79	
80		// ErrAdapterManifestMissingContractVersion is returned when the
81		// contract_version field is absent or empty.
82		ErrAdapterManifestMissingContractVersion = errors.New("adapter: adapter.yaml missing contract_version")
83	
84		// ErrAdapterContractVersionUnsupported is returned when
85		// contract_version is set but does not equal ContractVersionV1.
86		ErrAdapterContractVersionUnsupported = errors.New("adapter: contract_version not supported by this build")
87	
88		// ErrAdapterManifestEmptyCommand is returned when command is missing
89		// or an empty slice.
90		ErrAdapterManifestEmptyCommand = errors.New("adapter: adapter.yaml command is empty")
91	
92		// ErrAdapterManifestInvalidName is returned when name fails the
93		// adapter-id grammar.
94		ErrAdapterManifestInvalidName = errors.New("adapter: adapter.yaml name does not match required pattern")
95	
96		// ErrAdapterManifestInvalidReservedPrefix is returned when
97		// reserved_prefix is set but is not a clean, workspace-relative,
98		// forward-slash path. Rejected shapes: absolute paths (POSIX or
99		// Windows), Windows volume prefixes ("C:..."), backslashes (always
100		// reserved on POSIX, ambiguous on Windows), or any segment equal
101		// to ".." (workspace escape).
102		ErrAdapterManifestInvalidReservedPrefix = errors.New("adapter: reserved_prefix must be a clean workspace-relative forward-slash path")
103	)
104	
105	// adapterNamePattern enforces the same id grammar as IR nodes:
106	// leading alphanumeric, then up to 63 of [a-z0-9-_]. Total 1..64.
107	var adapterNamePattern = regexp.MustCompile(`\A[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,63}\z`)
108	
109	// LoadAdapterManifestFile reads adapter.yaml from disk and decodes it.
110	// File-size cap is enforced before the YAML parse so a hostile file
111	// cannot consume unbounded memory.
112	func LoadAdapterManifestFile(path string) (*AdapterManifest, error) {
113		info, err := os.Stat(path)
114		if err != nil {
115			return nil, fmt.Errorf("adapter: stat adapter.yaml: %w", err)
116		}
117		if info.Size() > MaxAdapterManifestBytes {
118			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: %d bytes > %d", ErrAdapterManifestTooLarge, info.Size(), MaxAdapterManifestBytes)
119		}
120		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
121		if err != nil {
122			return nil, fmt.Errorf("adapter: read adapter.yaml: %w", err)
123		}
124		if len(data) > MaxAdapterManifestBytes {
125			// Defense-in-depth: file could grow between stat and read.
126			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: %d bytes > %d", ErrAdapterManifestTooLarge, len(data), MaxAdapterManifestBytes)
127		}
128		return LoadAdapterManifestBytes(data)
129	}
130	
131	// LoadAdapterManifestBytes decodes a YAML byte slice and validates it.
132	// Strict mode: unknown non-x- fields are rejected; x-prefixed fields
133	// are accepted as forward-compatible extensions.
134	func LoadAdapterManifestBytes(src []byte) (*AdapterManifest, error) {
135		if len(src) > MaxAdapterManifestBytes {
136			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: %d bytes > %d", ErrAdapterManifestTooLarge, len(src), MaxAdapterManifestBytes)
137		}
138		var m AdapterManifest
139		if err := yaml.UnmarshalWithOptions(
140			src,
141			&m,
142			yaml.DisallowUnknownField(),
143			yaml.AllowFieldPrefixes("x-"),
144		); err != nil {
145			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: %s", ErrAdapterManifest, yaml.FormatError(err, false, true))
146		}
147		if err := validateAdapterManifest(&m); err != nil {
148			return nil, err
149		}
150		return &m, nil
151	}
152	
153	// SyntheticAdapterManifest constructs the implicit manifest used when
154	// PATH discovery finds an aienvs-adapter-<name> binary without a
155	// sibling adapter.yaml. Name must already be validated by the caller —
156	// adapter-id grammar is enforced via path matching upstream.
157	//
158	// binaryPath is the path the runtime should exec when spawning this
159	// adapter. Discovery passes the absolute path it resolved during
160	// directory scan (filepath.Join(dir, e.Name())) so spawn-time exec
161	// uses the exact binary discovery validated rather than re-resolving
162	// "aienvs-adapter-<name>" through $PATH (TOCTOU: the second lookup
163	// could pick up a different binary). When binaryPath is empty the
164	// manifest falls back to the bare PATH-relative name; callers without
165	// a resolved location only get the kubectl-plugin convention.
166	func SyntheticAdapterManifest(name, binaryPath string) *AdapterManifest {
167		cmd := binaryPath
168		if cmd == "" {
169			cmd = "aienvs-adapter-" + name
170		}
171		return &AdapterManifest{
172			Name:            name,
173			ContractVersion: ContractVersionV1,
174			Command:         []string{cmd},
175		}
176	}
177	
178	func validateAdapterManifest(m *AdapterManifest) error {
179		if !adapterNamePattern.MatchString(m.Name) {
180			return fmt.Errorf("%w: name=%q", ErrAdapterManifestInvalidName, m.Name)
181		}
182		if m.ContractVersion == "" {
183			return ErrAdapterManifestMissingContractVersion
184		}
185		if m.ContractVersion != ContractVersionV1 {
186			return fmt.Errorf("%w: %q (this build supports %q only)",
187				ErrAdapterContractVersionUnsupported, m.ContractVersion, ContractVersionV1)
188		}
189		if len(m.Command) == 0 {
190			return ErrAdapterManifestEmptyCommand
191		}
192		for i, arg := range m.Command {
193			if arg == "" {
194				return fmt.Errorf("%w: command[%d] is empty", ErrAdapterManifestEmptyCommand, i)
195			}
196		}
197		cleaned, err := ValidateReservedPrefix(m.ReservedPrefix)
198		if err != nil {
199			return err
200		}
201		m.ReservedPrefix = cleaned
202		return nil
203	}
204	
205	// ValidateReservedPrefix is the canonical validator for reserved_prefix
206	// values on both adapter.yaml and the workspace manifest's
207	// adapters[].reserved_prefix. It is a thin wrapper that maps the
208	// shared manifest.ValidateReservedPrefix sentinel onto the
209	// adapter-package sentinel so callers in this package can branch on
210	// errors.Is(err, ErrAdapterManifestInvalidReservedPrefix).
211	func ValidateReservedPrefix(s string) (string, error) {
212		cleaned, err := manifest.ValidateReservedPrefix(s)
213		if err != nil {
214			// Re-wrap with the adapter-package sentinel; preserve the
215			// underlying detail string for diagnostics.
216			return "", fmt.Errorf("%w: %s", ErrAdapterManifestInvalidReservedPrefix, errDetailAfterColon(err))
217		}
218		return cleaned, nil
219	}
220	
221	// errDetailAfterColon strips the leading "manifest: ..." sentinel prefix
222	// from a wrapped error so the adapter-side message reads naturally
223	// after re-wrapping. Falls back to err.Error() when the format doesn't
224	// match the expected shape.
225	func errDetailAfterColon(err error) string {
226		msg := err.Error()
227		if i := strings.Index(msg, ": "); i >= 0 {
228			return msg[i+2:]
229		}
230		return msg
231	}
232

488	## 13. Reserved for Unit 8b
489	
490	Not part of v1:
491	
492	- `$/cancelRequest`
493	- `$/progress`
494	- per-method timeouts in adapter manifests
495	- LSP extension error codes `-32800`, `-32801`, `-32803`
496	- version counter-proposal negotiation
497	
498	## 14. Canonical Examples
499	
500	The following examples are spec-locked. Each directive fence names the
501	matching corpus fixture under `internal/adapter/conformance/corpus/`.
502	Canonical examples in this spec are tagged with an
503	`aienvs:fixture-name` directive immediately preceding the JSON code
504	fence. The directive links a spec example to a corresponding corpus
505	fixture in `internal/adapter/conformance/corpus/`. The spec-locked test
506	in `internal/adapter/conformance/spec_locked_test.go` parses these
507	directives and asserts that the example payload exactly matches the
508	fixture's payload; drift in either direction fails CI.
509	
510	```aienvs:fixture-name
511	spec-example-handshake
512	```
513	```json
514	{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"client":"aienvs","protocol_versions":["aienvs/v1"],"cookie":"0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef","workspace_root":"/tmp/aienvs-conformance","reserved_prefix":".echo","ir_version":"v1"}}
515	```
516	
517	Canonical initialize result:
518	
519	```json
520	{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"result":{"server":"echo/0.1","protocol_version":"aienvs/v1","capabilities":{"concept_kinds":{"agents-md":"supported","rule":"supported","skill":"supported","command":"supported","plugin-reference":"supported","mcp-server-entry":"supported"},"write_tool_owned":true},"declared_outputs":[{"path":".echo","mode":"owned-subdir"}],"cookie":"0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef"}}
521	```
522	
523	```aienvs:fixture-name
524	spec-example-emit
525	```
526	```json
527	{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"emit","params":{"target":"spec-example-emit","ir":{"nodes":[{"id":"spec-emit","kind":"rule","body":"Adapters emit declarative ops."}]}}}
528	```
529	
530	Canonical emit result:
531	
532	```json
533	{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"result":{"ops_performed":[{"op":"mkdir","path":".echo"},{"op":"write_file","path":".echo/spec-emit.md"}]}}
534	```
535	
536	```aienvs:fixture-name
537	spec-example-error-response
538	```
539	```json
540	{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"error":{"code":-32602,"message":"adapter does not support any offered protocol version","data":{"error_class":"adapter-protocol-mismatch"}}}
541	```
542	
543	## 15. Conformance
544	
545	The frozen corpus lives under
546	`internal/adapter/conformance/corpus/`. The public CLI entry point is:
547	
548	```bash
549	aienvs adapter conformance-test ./my-adapter --format=json
550	```
551	
552	Default CLI behavior runs the positive corpus (`happy-*` plus
553	`spec-example-*`). Use `--include-adversarial` to also run `error-*`
554	fixtures; those require a hostile binary to pass and will fail against
555	an otherwise-correct adapter.
556	
557	The JSON output schema is:
558	
559	```json
560	{
561	  "version": "aienvs/v1",
562	  "cases": [
563	    {
564	      "name": "string",
565	      "status": "pass|fail|skip",
566	      "reason": "string",
567	      "expected_ops": [],
568	      "actual_ops": [],
569	      "missing_ops": [],
570	      "extra_ops": []
571	    }
572	  ],
573	  "summary": {
574	    "total": 0,
575	    "passed": 0,
576	    "failed": 0,
577	    "skipped": 0
578	  }
579	}
580	```
581	
582	## 16. CLI Reference
583	
584	`aienvs adapter conformance-test` exit codes:
585	
586	- `0`: all cases passed (skips do not count as failures)
587	- `1`: one or more cases failed

## 13. Reserved for Unit 8b

Not part of v1:

- `$/cancelRequest`
- `$/progress`
- per-method timeouts in adapter manifests
- LSP extension error codes `-32800`, `-32801`, `-32803`
- version counter-proposal negotiation

## 14. Canonical Examples

The following examples are spec-locked. Each directive fence names the
matching corpus fixture under `internal/adapter/conformance/corpus/`.
Canonical examples in this spec are tagged with an
`aienvs:fixture-name` directive immediately preceding the JSON code
fence. The directive links a spec example to a corresponding corpus
fixture in `internal/adapter/conformance/corpus/`. The spec-locked test
in `internal/adapter/conformance/spec_locked_test.go` parses these
directives and asserts that the example payload exactly matches the
fixture's payload; drift in either direction fails CI.

```aienvs:fixture-name
spec-example-handshake
```
```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"client":"aienvs","protocol_versions":["aienvs/v1"],"cookie":"0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef","workspace_root":"/tmp/aienvs-conformance","reserved_prefix":".echo","ir_version":"v1"}}
```

Canonical initialize result:

```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"result":{"server":"echo/0.1","protocol_version":"aienvs/v1","capabilities":{"concept_kinds":{"agents-md":"supported","rule":"supported","skill":"supported","command":"supported","plugin-reference":"supported","mcp-server-entry":"supported"},"write_tool_owned":true},"declared_outputs":[{"path":".echo","mode":"owned-subdir"}],"cookie":"0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef"}}
```

```aienvs:fixture-name
spec-example-emit
```
```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"emit","params":{"target":"spec-example-emit","ir":{"nodes":[{"id":"spec-emit","kind":"rule","body":"Adapters emit declarative ops."}]}}}
```

Canonical emit result:

```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"result":{"ops_performed":[{"op":"mkdir","path":".echo"},{"op":"write_file","path":".echo/spec-emit.md"}]}}
```

```aienvs:fixture-name
spec-example-error-response
```
```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"error":{"code":-32602,"message":"adapter does not support any offered protocol version","data":{"error_class":"adapter-protocol-mismatch"}}}
```

## 15. Conformance

The frozen corpus lives under
`internal/adapter/conformance/corpus/`. The public CLI entry point is:

```bash
aienvs adapter conformance-test ./my-adapter --format=json
```

Default CLI behavior runs the positive corpus (`happy-*` plus
`spec-example-*`). Use `--include-adversarial` to also run `error-*`
fixtures; those require a hostile binary to pass and will fail against
an otherwise-correct adapter.

The JSON output schema is:

```json
{
  "version": "aienvs/v1",
  "cases": [
    {
      "name": "string",
      "status": "pass|fail|skip",
      "reason": "string",
      "expected_ops": [],
      "actual_ops": [],
      "missing_ops": [],
      "extra_ops": []
    }
  ],
  "summary": {
    "total": 0,
    "passed": 0,
    "failed": 0,
    "skipped": 0
  }
}
```

## 16. CLI Reference

`aienvs adapter conformance-test` exit codes:

- `0`: all cases passed (skips do not count as failures)
- `1`: one or more cases failed

docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:102:6. **Filesystem safety — `os.Root` at the workspace-parent level [deepened].** Use **one `os.Root` scoped at a common ancestor** (typically `<workspace>/.claude/`, `<workspace>/.cursor/`, etc. — the parent of the reserved prefix) rather than one `os.Root` at the reserved prefix itself. Rationale (from Go 1.25 release notes + [Traversal-resistant file APIs](https://go.dev/blog/osroot)): on Windows, an open `os.Root` *handle holds its directory against rename* — a per-reserved-prefix `os.Root` would block its own swap. A parent-root also lets the staging directory live as a sibling (`<workspace>/.claude/.aienv-staging/<ts>/`) so step 1 (`aienvs/ → aienvs.old`) and step 2 (`.aienv-staging/<ts>/claude → aienvs`) happen inside a single root; Go 1.25 has no cross-root rename primitive ([proposal #73041](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/73041)). Generations root accordingly moves under each target's parent (see decision #20). Post-swap, per-reserved-prefix read-only `os.Root` handles are fine — the constraint applies only during the swap window.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:134:17. **`init` inside an enclosing Git worktree.** Detected automatically; `init` proposes concrete `.gitignore` entries (`.aienv/state/`, `.aienv/generations/`, `.aienv-staging/`, and any tool's `aienvs/` subtree the user doesn't want committed) and refuses unless accepted or `--no-gitignore-update` is passed.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:137:20. **Atomic-sync pipeline [deepened] — staging-under-target + syscall-truth error taxonomy.** Stage to `<workspace>/<target-parent>/.aienv-staging/<iso-timestamp>-<short-sha>/<target>/` (same parent directory as the live reserved prefix — e.g., `.claude/.aienv-staging/.../claude/` stages next to `.claude/aienvs/`). This makes cross-FS-by-construction impossible and lets a single parent `os.Root` (decision #6) span both step-1 and step-2 renames. Two-rename swap: `<prefix>` → `<prefix>.old`; `<staging>/<target>` → `<prefix>`; best-effort `RemoveAll <prefix>.old`. **Cross-FS detection uses rename-syscall result as truth, not a statfs pre-flight** (sources: [Linux bind mounts / btrfs subvols / overlayfs all return `EXDEV` despite matching `f_fsid`](https://serverfault.com/questions/327447), [macOS firmlinks cause `EXDEV` even within one APFS container](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/40351)). `statfs`/`FILE_ID_INFO` is used only to *enrich* error messages, never to gate the operation. **Error taxonomy** (surfaced as named Go error types): `ErrLocked` (Windows `SHARING_VIOLATION`/`ACCESS_DENIED` — retry up to 5× at 100ms/250ms/500ms/1s/2s with jitter; `--diagnose` opt-in calls Restart Manager enumeration to name the blocking process but **never** auto-`RmShutdown`), `ErrCrossVolume` (`EXDEV`/`ERROR_NOT_SAME_DEVICE` — abort, name both volumes, reference staging-under-target remediation), `ErrStale` (leftover `.old` or `.aienv-staging/*` from a prior crash — run recovery state machine), `ErrPermission` (non-retryable ACL). **Recovery state machine** persists a `<staging>/.state` sentinel with states `intend(ts)` → `step1_done(ts)` → `step2_done(ts)`; startup reconciles by rolling forward on `step1_done` (complete step 2) and rolling back on `intend` (discard staging). Retain the last 3 failed generations under `.aienv-staging/` for forensics; `aienvs sync --clean-scratch` force-clears. **Windows `os.Root` lifecycle:** any read-only per-reserved-prefix root must be closed before step 1 and reopened after step 2 — the parent-root (decision #6) is the only root that stays open across the swap window.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:138:21. **Required-unmet + capability report.** Capability report is assembled during staging and made available regardless of whether swap succeeds. On required_unmet failure, aienvs still writes the in-memory report to `.aienv/state/capability-report.json` so users can see what's missing even after atomic rollback. Best-effort does not weaken this — required-unmet is non-negotiable.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:194:│   ├── sync/                # staging, atomic swap, rollback, orphan+adopt
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:265:After staging succeeds:
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:641:- **Cancellation [Unit 8b].** `cancel` notification (LSP `$/cancelRequest` analog) carrying the JSON-RPC id of the in-flight `emit` request. **MVP:** on atomic-mode sibling failure, CLI sends `shutdown` to remaining adapters (not a mid-emit cancel) and discards their staged output — acceptable since staging is cheap and no user-visible file has been swapped.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:984:- [ ] **Unit 13: Atomic staging + swap + rollback — staging-under-target, single-parent `os.Root`, explicit recovery state machine**
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:986:**Goal:** Implement the two-rename swap pipeline with staging co-located under each target's parent directory, a single parent `os.Root` spanning both rename steps, syscall-truth cross-FS detection, a named error taxonomy, bounded Windows retry for `SHARING_VIOLATION`, opt-in Restart-Manager enumeration via `--diagnose`, and an explicit persisted recovery state machine.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:993:- Create: `internal/sync/staging.go` (per-target staging dir: `<workspace>/<target-parent>/.aienv-staging/<iso-ts>-<short-sha>/`)
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:1005:- **Staging location.** For each target `t` with reserved prefix `<workspace>/<target-parent>/aienvs/` (e.g., `.claude/rules/aienvs/`), stage at `<workspace>/<target-parent>/.aienv-staging/<iso-ts>-<short-sha>/<target>/` (a sibling of the live prefix under the same parent directory). **Invariant:** staging directory and live prefix share a parent, so a single parent `os.Root` (opened at `<workspace>/<target-parent>/`) scopes both rename steps. This makes cross-FS impossible by construction (staging is always on the same filesystem as the live prefix) and satisfies Go 1.25's no-cross-`Root`-rename constraint ([proposal #73041](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/73041)).
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:1006:- **Tool-owned-file targets** (decision #25 mode): these don't swap directories. Unit 13's swap logic applies only to reserved-subdirectory targets. Tool-owned-file writes use a per-file staging strategy: `<file>.aienv-tmp` → `rename` over `<file>` under a per-file lock (unit 12). Recovery for these is a separate sentinel in `.aienv/state/tool-owned-in-progress.json`.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:1008:  1. Write sentinel `<staging>/.state` = `{status:"intend", workspace, target, started_at, sha}`.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:1011:  4. `Rename(<staging>/<target-leaf>, <prefix>)` via parent `Root.Rename`.
docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:1015:- **Cross-FS detection is syscall-truth, not a pre-flight.** `os.Rename` returns `syscall.EXDEV` on Unix or `syscall.Errno(17) ERROR_NOT_SAME_DEVICE` on Windows when the operands cross filesystems. Rely on that return value exclusively (because: Linux bind-mounts, btrfs subvolumes, overlayfs, and macOS firmlinks all report matching `f_fsid`/device-id but still return `EXDEV` at rename time). `statfs`/`FILE_ID_INFO` is used only to *enrich* the `ErrCrossVolume` error message, never to gate the call. Staging-under-target (above) makes `ErrCrossVolume` unreachable in normal operation; if it ever surfaces it means the user has a bind-mount or submounted filesystem inside their workspace.

docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:102:6. **Filesystem safety — `os.Root` at the workspace-parent level [deepened].** Use **one `os.Root` scoped at a common ancestor** (typically `<workspace>/.claude/`, `<workspace>/.cursor/`, etc. — the parent of the reserved prefix) rather than one `os.Root` at the reserved prefix itself. Rationale (from Go 1.25 release notes + [Traversal-resistant file APIs](https://go.dev/blog/osroot)): on Windows, an open `os.Root` *handle holds its directory against rename* — a per-reserved-prefix `os.Root` would block its own swap. A parent-root also lets the staging directory live as a sibling (`<workspace>/.claude/.aienv-staging/<ts>/`) so step 1 (`aienvs/ → aienvs.old`) and step 2 (`.aienv-staging/<ts>/claude → aienvs`) happen inside a single root; Go 1.25 has no cross-root rename primitive ([proposal #73041](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/73041)). Generations root accordingly moves under each target's parent (see decision #20). Post-swap, per-reserved-prefix read-only `os.Root` handles are fine — the constraint applies only during the swap window.

This makes cross-FS-by-construction impossible and lets a single parent `os.Root` (decision #6) span both step-1 and step-2 renames. Two-rename swap: `<prefix>` → `<prefix>.old`; `<staging>/<target>` → `<prefix>`; best-effort `RemoveAll <prefix>.old`. **Cross-FS detection uses rename-syscall result as truth, not a statfs pre-flight** (sources: [Linux bind mounts / btrfs subvols / overlayfs all return `EXDEV` despite matching `f_fsid`](https://serverfault.com/questions/327447), [macOS firmlinks cause `EXDEV` even within one APFS container](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/40351)). `statfs`/`FILE_ID_INFO` is used only to *enrich* error messages, never to gate the operation. **Error taxonomy** (surfaced as named Go error types): `ErrLocked` (Windows `SHARING_VIOLATION`/`ACCESS_DENIED` — retry up to 5× at 100ms/250ms/500ms/1s/2s with jitter; `--diagnose` opt-in calls Restart Manager enumeration to name the blocking process but **never** auto-`RmShutdown`), `ErrCrossVolume` (`EXDEV`/`ERROR_NOT_SAME_DEVICE` — abort, name both volumes, reference staging-under-target remediation), `ErrStale` (leftover `.old` or `.aienv-staging/*` from a prior crash — run recovery state machine), `ErrPermission` (non-retryable ACL). **Recovery state machine** persists a `<staging>/.state` sentinel with states `intend(ts)` → `step1_done(ts)` → `step2_done(ts)`; startup reconciles by rolling forward on `step1_done` (complete step 2) and rolling back on `intend` (discard staging). Retain the last 3 failed generations under `.aienv-staging/` for forensics; `aienvs sync --clean-scratch` force-clears. **Windows `os.Root` lifecycle:** any read-only per-reserved-prefix root must be closed before step 1 and reopened after step 2 — the parent-root (decision #6) is the only root that stays open across the swap window.

On required_unmet failure, aienvs still writes the in-memory report to `.aienv/state/capability-report.json` so users can see what's missing even after atomic rollback. # Go cross-platform CI pitfalls — build tags, filepath.IsAbs, and path-separator comparisons

## Context

Cross-platform Go CI revealed three distinct compile-time and test-time failures when a Windows matrix was exercised against a repo that had only been validated on Linux/macOS. All three failures share a single underlying principle: the assumption that a runtime guard (`if runtime.GOOS == "windows" { t.Skip() }`) provides the same protection as a structural or build-time guarantee. It does not. Runtime checks execute at test time; build tags and path-normalization are enforced earlier — at compile time and at the type level, respectively. When these are confused, code that looks safe on Unix silently breaks the moment a different OS tries to compile or run it.

A precedent for the correct pattern already existed in this repo: Unit 1's `internal/fsroot` package split syscall-dependent code into `_unix.go` / `_windows.go` / `_other.go` files from the start (`samefs_unix.go` + `samefs_windows.go`, `nofollow_unix.go` + `nofollow_other.go`). That discipline was not applied to new tests in Units 3–4, and the gap only surfaced when the GitHub Actions matrix ran Windows for the first time. (session history)

## Guidance

### Rule 1: Use build tags for files that import platform-specific packages

`t.Skip` is a runtime operation. If a file unconditionally imports a package that does not exist on a target platform, the compiler rejects the entire file before any test logic runs. The skip is never reached.

**Before** — compiles fine on Linux, fails `go vet` on Windows:

```go
// internal/workspace/discover_test.go
import (
    "runtime"
    "syscall" // syscall.Mkfifo does not exist on Windows
)

func TestFind_ExplicitWorkspaceToFIFO(t *testing.T) {
    if runtime.GOOS == "windows" {
        t.Skip("FIFOs are not available on Windows") // never reached — file won't compile
    }
    if err := syscall.Mkfifo(fifoPath, 0o644); err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("mkfifo: %v", err)
    }
}
```

**After** — split into a build-tagged file, no runtime skip required:

```go
// internal/workspace/discover_unix_test.go

It does not. Runtime checks execute at test time; build tags and path-normalization are enforced earlier — at compile time and at the type level, respectively. When these are confused, code that looks safe on Unix silently breaks the moment a different OS tries to compile or run it.

A precedent for the correct pattern already existed in this repo: Unit 1's `internal/fsroot` package split syscall-dependent code into `_unix.go` / `_windows.go` / `_other.go` files from the start (`samefs_unix.go` + `samefs_windows.go`, `nofollow_unix.go` + `nofollow_other.go`). That discipline was not applied to new tests in Units 3–4, and the gap only surfaced when the GitHub Actions matrix ran Windows for the first time. (session history)

## Guidance

### Rule 1: Use build tags for files that import platform-specific packages

`t.Skip` is a runtime operation. If a file unconditionally imports a package that does not exist on a target platform, the compiler rejects the entire file before any test logic runs. The skip is never reached.

**Before** — compiles fine on Linux, fails `go vet` on Windows:

```go
// internal/workspace/discover_test.go
import (
    "runtime"
    "syscall" // syscall.Mkfifo does not exist on Windows
)

func TestFind_ExplicitWorkspaceToFIFO(t *testing.T) {
    if runtime.GOOS == "windows" {
        t.Skip("FIFOs are not available on Windows") // never reached — file won't compile
    }
    if err := syscall.Mkfifo(fifoPath, 0o644); err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("mkfifo: %v", err)
    }
}
```

**After** — split into a build-tagged file, no runtime skip required:

```go
// internal/workspace/discover_unix_test.go

94	
95	The requirements doc left several technical details to planning. All resolved here; nothing is a hidden unknown. *Decisions marked [deepened] were tightened during the 2026-04-21 confidence pass with cited research — see Sources & References.*
96	
97	1. **Go 1.25 minimum.** `os.Root` + CVE-2025-0913 fix + cross-platform reparse-point detection require it. Rationale: filesystem safety is the highest-risk surface; accepting an older toolchain means rebuilding this from scratch.
98	2. **CLI framework: Cobra v1.9 + Fang v2.** Largest ecosystem, shell completions + man pages for free, pairs cleanly with Bubble Tea. Kong and urfave/cli evaluated; Cobra wins on ecosystem depth for a 10+ subcommand surface.
99	3. **TUI: Bubble Tea v2 + bubbles v2 + lipgloss v2 + huh v2** for wizards, with a small hand-rolled stack navigator for the multi-screen init flow. Accessibility: `huh.WithAccessible` + honor `NO_COLOR`.
100	4. **Git materialization: hybrid.** Shell out to `git` for all network operations (auth, credential helpers, SSH config, LFS, sparse checkout — things go-git can't do reliably). Use go-git v5.17 only for read-only operations on the cached clone. Keep the seam narrow so pure-go can replace the shell-out later if go-git's credential story improves.
101	5. **YAML: goccy/go-yaml v1.19.x** with `DisallowUnknownField()` + `AllowFieldPrefixes("x-")` for forward-compat extension keys. `CommentMap` used when `init` writes the resolved SHA back into the manifest.
102	6. **Filesystem safety — `os.Root` at the workspace-parent level [deepened].** Use **one `os.Root` scoped at a common ancestor** (typically `<workspace>/.claude/`, `<workspace>/.cursor/`, etc. — the parent of the reserved prefix) rather than one `os.Root` at the reserved prefix itself. Rationale (from Go 1.25 release notes + [Traversal-resistant file APIs](https://go.dev/blog/osroot)): on Windows, an open `os.Root` *handle holds its directory against rename* — a per-reserved-prefix `os.Root` would block its own swap. A parent-root also lets the staging directory live as a sibling (`<workspace>/.claude/.aienv-staging/<ts>/`) so step 1 (`aienvs/ → aienvs.old`) and step 2 (`.aienv-staging/<ts>/claude → aienvs`) happen inside a single root; Go 1.25 has no cross-root rename primitive ([proposal #73041](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/73041)). Generations root accordingly moves under each target's parent (see decision #20). Post-swap, per-reserved-prefix read-only `os.Root` handles are fine — the constraint applies only during the swap window.
103	7. **File locking: `gofrs/flock` v0.13** with `TryLockContext` + bounded timeout. One lock per (workspace, target); cache directory has its own.
104	8. **Adapter extension contract [deepened]: LSP-style `Content-Length` framing + JSON-RPC 2.0 body** over stdin/stdout, protocol identifier `aienvs/v1` carried in `Content-Type: application/aienvs-v1+json; charset=utf-8`. Rationale ([LSP 3.17 base protocol](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/), [MCP 2025-11-25 lifecycle](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/latest/basic/lifecycle)): length-prefixed headers are 8-bit-clean, debug-friendly (`cat`/`tee`/`tail`), survive partial writes, and are the de-facto standard adopted by every JSON-over-stdio protocol shipped by Microsoft, Anthropic, and HashiCorp-ish successors. Bundled adapters (claude/cursor/codex/pi) still run via in-process shim but speak the same wire protocol for parity. **Required v1 surface before freeze** (each item sourced from prior-art gap analysis, see Sources):
105	    - Transport: `Content-Length:`/`Content-Type:` headers + JSON-RPC 2.0 request/response/notification envelope.
106	    - **`initialize` → `initialized`** lifecycle (LSP/MCP): server must not emit real ops before receiving `initialized` notification.
107	    - **Server counter-proposes version** on mismatch (MCP rule): adapter replies with its highest supported protocol, CLI decides to proceed or refuse.
108	    - **`capabilities` object** in the initialize result so per-concept support flags evolve without bumping `v1`→`v2`.
109	    - **`declared_outputs` list** in the initialize result (Bazel `declare_file` pattern): adapter announces its full planned output set upfront so the CLI can detect undeclared writes mid-stream and refuse.
110	    - **`cancel` notification** with JSON-RPC id (analog of LSP `$/cancelRequest`) and **`shutdown`** request (analog of Terraform `Stop`) before SIGTERM.
111	    - **Progress tokens** on long-running emits (LSP `WorkDoneProgress` begin/report/end), so a crashed adapter is reported as "exited after N/M planned writes" rather than silent hang.
112	    - **Per-op timeouts** with sensible defaults (5s handshake, 30s per op — matching k8s mutating-webhook conventions), configurable per adapter via `adapter.yaml`.
113	    - **Structured error envelope** using JSON-RPC 2.0 error codes plus LSP-specific extensions (`-32800 RequestCancelled`, `-32801 ContentModified`, `-32803 RequestFailed`) so callers can branch on machine-readable `code`.
114	    - **Magic-cookie handshake** (copying `hashicorp/go-plugin`): CLI sets an env var per spawn and the adapter's `Hello`/`initialize` must echo it, so running `./aienvs-adapter-foo` directly at a shell refuses.
115	    - **Reserved `_meta` object** on every frame (MCP convention) for forward-compatible metadata additions.
116	    - **Stdout reserved for framed bytes only** (LSP rule); stderr is free-form diagnostic channel that the CLI captures and surfaces in failure reports.
117	   Rejected alternatives: `hashicorp/go-plugin` gRPC (heavier than needed, Go-biased); length-delimited raw JSON with no headers (no content-type, no charset tagging); NDJSON (bans embedded newlines); Go stdlib `plugin` (cross-platform-broken); WASM/wazero (Go→WASI tooling immature in 2026).
118	   Discovery unchanged: `aienvs-adapter-<name>` binary on `PATH` or explicit `adapters:` entry in manifest; bundled primaries compiled in but also installable as external binaries so third parties see a complete working example. **Declarative-only output** remains the wire contract (CLI performs all writes inside the parent `os.Root`), but is documented as *not a security boundary* against hostile adapters — a malicious subprocess can always fork and write directly; supply-chain trust (TOFU + SHA pin at install time) is the real defense.
119	9. **TOFU — committed trust sidecar as primary CI pattern [deepened]** (replaces the per-invocation `--accept-new-source=<sha>` model). Two stores with two purposes:
120	    - **Per-project pin (authoritative for CI):** `trusted_sha:` field in `.aienv.yaml`, committed to the repo alongside `commit:`. Sync in non-interactive/CI mode requires `trusted_sha` to match the resolved SHA exactly; any drift is a hard failure with no prompt. This is the `npm ci` / `go.sum` pattern — trust is established once on a human's machine, enforced mechanically in CI. `aienvs trust pin` is the subcommand that writes `trusted_sha:` into the manifest; `aienvs trust verify` is the read-only CI gate.
121	    - **Per-user history (authoritative for interactive cross-project recall):** `trust.jsonl` append-only log at `xdg.DataHome()/aienvs/trust.jsonl`, mode 0600. Schema: `{"ts","op","url","sha","prev_sha","source","actor","hostname"}` where `op ∈ {trust, promote, revoke, allow-new-shas-on, allow-new-shas-off}`. Current state is the fold over the log (`aienvs trust status`), matching SSH `known_hosts`/`go.sum` precedent. `aienvs trust compact` rotates oversized logs. **No HMAC/cryptographic integrity in v1** — document honestly that the store is only as trustworthy as the user account, and defend the supply-chain surface at `trusted_sha` instead.
122	    Three mismatch classes with **distinct error classes and exit codes** (from [SSH/go.sum/gh-attestation UX comparison](https://blog.gitguardian.com/renovate-dependabot-the-new-malware-delivery-system/)):
123	    - `RevokedTrustAnchor` (exit 3) — SSH-style red banner, **no prompt regardless of TTY**, names the offending file + remediation (`aienvs trust reset <url>`).
124	    - `TrustDecisionRequired` (exit 4) — first-URL or SHA-update in a non-TTY context: exits immediately with a flag hint (`rerun with --accept-new-source=<sha> or commit trusted_sha`). Never hangs on a prompt.
125	    - `FirstUseDenied` (exit 5) — user declined the interactive prompt.
126	    Interactive SHA-update **no longer prompts inside the sync flow**; instead sync emits a one-line reminder and a new **`aienvs trust pending` / `trust diff` / `trust promote`** batch-review command handles promotions out of band (Dependabot/Renovate pattern — trust decisions are audit-grade review, not hot-path friction). `trust-all-new-shas` gains an optional **cooldown** (`minimumReleaseAge`, Renovate pattern) instead of being a raw bypass.
127	10. **`--accept-new-source` is SHA-scoped and audited [deepened].** Flag shape unchanged: `--accept-new-source=<sha>` / `--accept-new-source=<url>:<sha>`. Every successful use echoes `Trusting new source: URL=<url> SHA=<sha>` to **stderr** before proceeding (gh audit-trail principle — disable the prompt, not the announcement). `--accept-new-source=any` now requires a **peer gate**: either `AIENVS_ALLOW_UNSAFE_ANY=1` in env *or* `--i-understand-this-is-dangerous` on the command line. This follows the [gh#12033 lesson](https://github.com/cli/cli/issues/12033) that single-flag safety overrides produce recurring bug reports. `--yes` / non-interactive flags are no-ops without an explicit resource argument.
128	11. **`--adopt-prefix` is backed by a backup tarball.** Before adoption writes to the ledger, aienvs writes `.aienv/state/backups/<target>-<timestamp>.tar.gz` containing everything in the reserved prefix. Adoption then proceeds. Interactive flow requires **typed target name** (`claude`, not `y`) per gh/Stripe CLI convention. `--adopt-prefix-no-backup` exists for users who understand. All-or-nothing per prefix; selective preservation requires manual file-move first. Symmetric destructive command `aienvs trust revoke <url>` also requires typed-name confirmation.
129	12. **Mid-life drift uses the same guard.** If between syncs a user creates a file inside a reserved prefix not tracked by the ledger, `sync` refuses identically to the first-sync guard. Invariant: the ledger-or-adopt rule holds for the entire lifetime of the workspace, not just on first sync.
130	13. **Ledger missing/corrupted/schema-drift.** Missing or JSON-corrupted ledger triggers the first-sync-guard path (everything in reserved prefix treated as user content needing adoption). Older-schema ledger requires `--migrate-state` or interactive confirm; silent auto-migration is forbidden. Ledger records **content hash** per emitted path so hand-edit drift is detectable by `validate`.
131	14. **`validate` semantics.** Shares `sync`'s network posture: online if floating + network; cache-only if pinned + cached; fails offline unless `--offline`. Flags `--no-fetch` / `--fetch` override. Exit codes: `0` no drift, `1` drift detected (clean), `2+` operational error. `--output=json` mirrors sync summary schema plus a `diff` node. Capability report assembled in-memory for reporting but **not written to disk** (preserves read-only posture). `--diff` adds unified-diff body for text files.
132	15. **Symlink-walk terminus & cycle safety.** Walk-up terminates at **filesystem root** by default (picks up `~/.aienv.yaml`). `--workspace-stop-at=<path>` and `AIENVS_WORKSPACE_STOP_AT` env var override. Bounded-hop cycle safety: refuse after 64 parent traversals (documented) — full inode-based cycle detection remains deferred per origin R8.

135	18. **`init` rejects credentials in URL.** `https://user:token@host/…` surfaces a specific error with a documented remediation (git credential helpers / SSH). Cache-key canonicalization strips userinfo regardless.
136	19. **Ancestor-workspace nesting policy.** `init` detects an existing `.aienv.yaml` up the tree; requires `--allow-nested` or interactive confirm to proceed. Message names both paths so shadowing is intentional.
137	20. **Atomic-sync pipeline [deepened] — staging-under-target + syscall-truth error taxonomy.** Stage to `<workspace>/<target-parent>/.aienv-staging/<iso-timestamp>-<short-sha>/<target>/` (same parent directory as the live reserved prefix — e.g., `.claude/.aienv-staging/.../claude/` stages next to `.claude/aienvs/`). This makes cross-FS-by-construction impossible and lets a single parent `os.Root` (decision #6) span both step-1 and step-2 renames. Two-rename swap: `<prefix>` → `<prefix>.old`; `<staging>/<target>` → `<prefix>`; best-effort `RemoveAll <prefix>.old`. **Cross-FS detection uses rename-syscall result as truth, not a statfs pre-flight** (sources: [Linux bind mounts / btrfs subvols / overlayfs all return `EXDEV` despite matching `f_fsid`](https://serverfault.com/questions/327447), [macOS firmlinks cause `EXDEV` even within one APFS container](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/40351)). `statfs`/`FILE_ID_INFO` is used only to *enrich* error messages, never to gate the operation. **Error taxonomy** (surfaced as named Go error types): `ErrLocked` (Windows `SHARING_VIOLATION`/`ACCESS_DENIED` — retry up to 5× at 100ms/250ms/500ms/1s/2s with jitter; `--diagnose` opt-in calls Restart Manager enumeration to name the blocking process but **never** auto-`RmShutdown`), `ErrCrossVolume` (`EXDEV`/`ERROR_NOT_SAME_DEVICE` — abort, name both volumes, reference staging-under-target remediation), `ErrStale` (leftover `.old` or `.aienv-staging/*` from a prior crash — run recovery state machine), `ErrPermission` (non-retryable ACL). **Recovery state machine** persists a `<staging>/.state` sentinel with states `intend(ts)` → `step1_done(ts)` → `step2_done(ts)`; startup reconciles by rolling forward on `step1_done` (complete step 2) and rolling back on `intend` (discard staging). Retain the last 3 failed generations under `.aienv-staging/` for forensics; `aienvs sync --clean-scratch` force-clears. **Windows `os.Root` lifecycle:** any read-only per-reserved-prefix root must be closed before step 1 and reopened after step 2 — the parent-root (decision #6) is the only root that stays open across the swap window.

Best-effort does not weaken this — required-unmet is non-negotiable.

143	    - **Reserved-subdirectory mode** (preferred): adapter owns `.<tool>/<subsystem>/aienvs/` as a namespace. Verified viable for Claude/Cursor rules (`.claude/rules/aienvs/<id>.md`, `.cursor/rules/aienvs/<id>.mdc`) and Claude commands (`.claude/commands/aienvs/<id>.md`, which get free `/aienvs:<id>` slash-command namespacing). Orphan removal edits the file in place, preserving non-aienvs entries. Emit uses a read-merge-write pattern under a per-file lock; the ledger's content hash covers the aienvs-owned slice, not the whole file.
145	    The adapter framework (unit 8) exposes a `write_tool_owned` op distinct from `write_file` so this mode is first-class rather than a workaround. The per-tool concept→destination mapping is authoritative in each adapter's `docs/adapters/<tool>.md` and encoded in its `capabilities.yaml`. 226	## High-Level Technical Design
227	
228	> *This illustrates the intended approach and is directional guidance for review, not implementation specification. The implementing agent should treat it as context, not code to reproduce.*
229	
230	### Pipeline
231	
232	```mermaid
233	flowchart LR
234	    M[.aienv.yaml] --> D[Discover workspace]
235	    D --> R[Resolve canonical source]
236	    R -->|network + TOFU| C[(Cache: deterministic SHA-256 keyed)]
237	    C --> I[Compile to IR v1]
238	    I --> A1[Adapter: claude]
239	    I --> A2[Adapter: cursor]
240	    I --> A3[Adapter: codex]
241	    I --> A4[Adapter: out-of-tree ...]
242	    A1 --> S1[Stage in .aienv/generations/.../claude/]
243	    A2 --> S2[Stage in .aienv/generations/.../cursor/]
244	    A3 --> S3[Stage in .aienv/generations/.../codex/]
245	    A4 --> SN[Stage ...]
246	    S1 & S2 & S3 & SN --> G{All OK?}
247	    G -->|yes, atomic default| SWAP[Two-rename swap per target]
248	    G -->|no, atomic| RB[Discard generation, keep prior]
249	    G -->|best-effort| PARTIAL[Swap succeeded; skip failed]
250	    SWAP --> L[Write ledgers + capability report]
251	    PARTIAL --> L
252	    RB --> CR[Write capability report only]
253	    L --> SUM[Per-target summary]
254	    CR --> SUM
255	```
256	
257	### Generation-directory swap (per target)
258	
259	```
260	Before sync:
261	  ~/ws/.claude/aienvs/                  <-- live reserved prefix
262	  ~/ws/.aienv/state/claude.json         <-- live ledger (hashed paths)
263	  ~/ws/.aienv/generations/2026-.../claude/ <-- staged scratch (same FS as prefix)
264	
265	After staging succeeds:
266	  rename ~/ws/.claude/aienvs        -> ~/ws/.claude/aienvs.old
267	  rename ~/ws/.aienv/generations/.../claude -> ~/ws/.claude/aienvs
268	  RemoveAll ~/ws/.claude/aienvs.old (best-effort)
269	```
270	
271	Recovery: on startup if `.old` is found, check the ledger — if the new ledger matches the new prefix contents, finish cleanup; otherwise, swap back to `.old` and report recovery to the user.
